Let me just tell you something. This is what typically happens in most companies out there. They spend all this time trying to land a new customer, and then once they actually land the customer, all that wooing, all that value they probably just goes out the window. And then they start developing this pattern of customer churn, which we have to keep advertising, going through the same funnel again to land customer. So it's just a destructive thing to do. So now that I've framed what the value, really, really important, so creating customers for life, let me tell you exactly what the phrase means, and then I'm actually going to go through, as I always do, get very, very specific here, and I'm going to go through 10 strategies, maybe a couple of bonus strategies as well. And if we have time, we're going to go through the referral site. We can't get to it this time, I'll deal with it another time yet, but I'm going to get very deep now, and go through the actual things that you do to ensure that you take a one-time customer and turn them into a raving fan. And this jibes very directly to those of you who do the daily motivations, you get the daily motivations from me every day, right? A separate program, okay? And I don't want to talk about every site here, so I want to talk about that, but for those of you I spoke about this once, and they call it the step down sale. So let me do a quick refresh everyone's recollection about what that is you all have with the straight line system. But let's go through very quick, right? The straight line is such, right? This is your open and this is your closed, right? We have our healthy boundaries above and below the line. When you're inside these boundaries, you're in control. When you're outside these boundaries, you're on Pluto or you're in this. And remember, this is a very important distinction here, the essence of going to Pluto is when you ask a prospect a question, and they typically will start answering your question in the way you want me, and they'll give you relevant information. But somewhere along the way, they start taking a left turn to Albuquerque, and next thing you know, you're talking about something that's completely unrelated to the subject, hey, if you look at personal life, your family, stuff that's just simply not germane to opening up a new account. That's death to the sales process. This idea of going off to Pluto and talking about things that are not germane to the sale. It's a telltale sign of a novice. You waste your time, you waste the prospect's time, and it does not build rapport like people think it's actually repulsive to people. That being said, however, that rule actually flips up on its end after you close the deal. So one of the mistakes people make with this trail is after they close a sale, that rule's gone. Now that they're actually your client, you actually want to spend a bit of time in Pluto because you want to identify certain aspects of their personal life and things that are exactly germane to the sale to let the customer know that you really care. And that's not unprofessional. It's professional and shows that you're not just there to make a sale. But let me stand the straight line right now. So you go down the straight line, you establish yourself as an expert shop, for a short couple of seconds, you gather your intelligence, you build investment before, you do transition, you then do your straight line sales for instance, presentation, which ended you asking for the order for the first time, and then you go through your looping patterns, right? And here's what I mean by a step down sale. Typically what we do in the straight line, when we can, it's usually possible, is that we ask for a very big order the first time. The purest example is in the stock market, where we're in the straight line, you might say it's on pick up 10,000 shares of $50 stock, right, which is a $500,000 trade. That's a lot of money for someone to send to someone they've met before, they've spoken to before, right, to stranger. So you ask them for a $500,000 commitment, and they say, let me think about it. And then you engage in your looping, and then you ask for the order the second time, but the second time instead of asking for $500,000, you say, well, why don't we just have a slide over here, let's go on a bit small, we'll pick up, you know, let's pick up only, you know, let's pick up 2,000 shares. We're talking about a cash out, we have only $100,000 right now, of course you'll make less money as the stock trades higher. In other words, what I'm saying, what the percentage gain rate is saying, what I'm doing here is essentially, each time I run a loop, I reduce the level of commitment. So if you're an insurance salesman, try to land somebody on a major insurance package with all these different types of policy, like disability, life, hell right. As you went down the line and was asking for the order again, you might try, and you would try, to essentially lower the amount of the entry. So instead of it costing or being an investment of $100,000, you could start with $10,000. If you're selling someone a massive home video system, you could say to them, why don't you start with smaller, just work on this room first here, we'll do the main living room here, we'll do the whole wide open, we can work after you see how much you love it, how great it works, we can come back and do the rest of the job down the road. Does that sound fair enough? So as you loop, you keep lowering them, the amount, lowering the amount, lowering the amount, right? So eventually the last policy you run for loops, when you ask for the order the final time, you essentially be at the firm minimum, the lowest level, the lowest amount of money that you could essentially open up in a cabinet, whatever the least that makes sense in your industry. So you start off asking for a very big order, and you end up with a very small order, right? That whole premise of the straight line, is you give me a shot, let me show you what I can do, let me earn your trust, and then down the road, once you see how awesome my product is, once you see what great customer service I give, once you see how much I can do for you, how well my company stands behind the front, once you see all of that, then we can work bigger and better down the road. And if you do that, believe me, the only problem you'll have is that you didn't buy more. Does that sound fair enough? That's your basic premise of the straight line, in a nutshell, right? And in one context, right? However, that does not work very well if after you close the deal, you don't ever build a relationship with the client. If you don't get that client who is essentially testing out your idea, you don't turn that person into a raving fan. You get it? So that's gotta be just as important as learning how to open up an account.
